metadata.title: Longitudinal analysis of high-risk HPV infections reveals within-host viral genome changes over time
metadata.description:
This repository contains patient phenotype data, HPV genomic variant data, HPV consensus sequences, and sequenced HPV reads aligned to high-risk HPV reference genomes generated as part of the study “Longitudinal analysis of high-risk HPV infections reveals within-host viral genome changes over time”.

The high-risk HPV reference genomes and the pipeline used to process the sequencing data can be found at https://github.com/NCI-CGR/HPV_Guanacaste_CostaRica_Longitudinal_Study.

To facilitate upload and download of the large sequencing dataset, the BAM files deposited in this repository were packaged into compressed archive files, which were subsequently split into multiple parts.

After downloading all parts from this repository, users should first concatenate the split files to reconstruct the original compressed archives using the following commands:

cat bam_files_set1_part_* > bam_files_set1.tar.gz

cat bam_files_set2_part_* > bam_files_set2.tar.gz

Once the original compressed archives have been reconstructed, the individual BAM files can be extracted using:

tar -zxvf bam_files_set1.tar.gz

The same command can be used to extract files from bam_files_set2.tar.gz.

metadata.title: Data for:  Viral Inactivation Process Monitoring by APIES (Absorbance, Polarized Intrinsic Emission, and light Scattering): simultaneous protein aggregation, concentration, and stability analysis
metadata.description:
This is the raw data used for the manuscript:  Viral Inactivation Process Monitoring by APIES (Absorbance, Polarized Intrinsic Emission, and light Scattering): simultaneous protein aggregation, concentration, and stability analysis.  Published online 22/06/2026

https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.6c00640

The data is organised into a series of Excel files and includes DLS data tables, UV absorbance values, SEC, and 2D fluorescence emisison spectra.  

Please email the authors for access to these files. 

Jared Zang:  J.zang1@universityofgalway.ie

Alan Ryder:  alan.ryder@universityofgalway.ie

metadata.title: Comparison of zinc levels in liver cirrhosis and evaluating the severity using child pugh score
metadata.description:
Background and Objectives:  
 “Liver cirrhosis represents the final common pathway for chronic liver 
diseases, characterized by extensive fibrosis and hepatocyte dysfunction. Zinc, an 
essential micronutrient with critical roles in protein synthesis, enzymatic reactions, 
and antioxidant defense, has been implicated in liver pathophysiology. However, the 
relationship between zinc deficiency and cirrhosis severity remains incompletely 
characterized in the Indian population. This study aimed to evaluate serum zinc levels 
in patients with liver cirrhosis and correlate them with disease severity as measured 
by the Child-Pugh classification.” 
Methods:  
 “This hospital-based cross-sectional study was conducted among 85 patients 
with cirrhosis of liver  attending the outpatient and inpatient departments of BLDE 
University's Shri BM Patil Medical College Hospital. Detailed clinical evaluation, 
biochemical investigations including serum zinc levels, and Child-Pugh scoring were 
performed. Zinc deficiency was defined as serum levels below 51 μg/dL. Statistical 
analysis included descriptive statistics, chi-square tests, and ANOVA to assess 
relationships between variables.” 
Results:  
 The study cohort comprised predominantly middle-aged males (95.3%), with 
alcoholic etiology (89.4%) being the leading cause of cirrhosis. Advanced disease 
was common, with 67.1% of patients categorized as Child-Pugh Class C. Zinc 
deficiency was observed in 97.6% of patients, with mean zinc levels showing a 
Docusign Envelope ID: 5C7BDBEB-49C3-48DF-A0F7-1CD930E1B711
 
 
X  
significant progressive decrease from Child-Pugh Class A (50.4±4.31 μg/dL) to Class 
B (42.32±4.86 μg/dL) to Class C (37.02±3.68 μg/dL) (p<0.001). There was a perfect 
parallelism between zinc and albumin deficiency (both 97.6%). The mortality rate 
during the study period was 15.3%. 
Conclusion:  
 This study demonstrates an extraordinarily high prevalence of zinc deficiency 
in liver cirrhosis with a significant inverse correlation with disease severity. The 
progressive decline in zinc levels with worsening Child-Pugh scores suggests that 
zinc deficiency may be both a marker of advanced disease and potentially a 
contributor to disease progression. These findings support the incorporation of zinc 
assessment into the routine evaluation of cirrhotic patients and suggest that zinc 
supplementation could be considered as an adjunctive therapy, particularly in 
advanced disease.

metadata.title: rneat (rusty-neat): a Rust toolkit for simulating NGS read data, including tumor/normal cancer sequencing
metadata.description: rneat is a Rust port and extension of NEAT that generates simulated next-generation sequencing data in FASTQ and VCF formats with the statistical properties of real datasets, along with a golden BAM/VCF truth set of inserted variants and ideal alignments. It adds structural-variant simulation (CNV, BND, INV, INS, and others) and an end-to-end tumor/normal cancer workflow (gen-cancer-reads) that merges two read passes at a configurable purity and emits an origin-tagged truth VCF for benchmarking somatic and structural-variant callers, with an emphasis on low memory use and short CPU time.

metadata.title: VirProtRAG: Literature-grounded viral protein function annotation with retrieval-augmented generation
metadata.description: VirProtRAG is a viral protein function annotation framework that reformulates the task as an integrated process of information retrieval and evidence‑grounded knowledge generation. VirProtRAG introduces three task-adapted components: (i) a hybrid retrieval module combining keyword‑based and semantic dense retrieval to maximize literature coverage, (ii) synonym‑expanded, rank‑aware retrieval with reciprocal rank fusion for improved search effectiveness, and (iii) literature quality and evidence‑oriented re‑ranking to enhance reliability and interpretability.

data.title: VirProtRAG: Literature-grounded viral protein function annotation with retrieval-augmented generation

metadata.title: Atypical Presentation of Molluscum Contagiosum on the Thorax: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Approach
metadata.description: Molluscum contagiosum is a common viral skin infection in the pediatric population caused by a virus belonging to the Poxviridae family, characterized by the appearance of progressively growing umbilicated pearly papules. Although it commonly affects the face, extremities, and trunk, extensive or atypical forms may represent a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. We present the case of an 8-year-old immunocompetent male patient with multiple lesions predominantly located on the thoracic region with four months of evolution, associated with pruritus and progressive dissemination. Physical examination revealed multiple pearly hemispheric papules with central umbilication and grouped distribution on the anterior and right lateral thorax. Due to the unusual clinical presentation, dermoscopy and skin biopsy were performed, confirming the diagnosis of molluscum contagiosum through histopathological findings compatible with intracytoplasmic Henderson-Patterson bodies. Treatment consisted of selective curettage of the main lesions and complementary topical therapy associated with hygienic and dermatological measures to reduce autoinoculation. The patient showed satisfactory clinical evolution, adequate healing, and progressive resolution of the lesions without major complications. This case highlights the importance of considering molluscum contagiosum among the differential diagnoses of thoracic papular lesions in pediatric patients, as well as the usefulness of clinical and histopathological approaches for establishing timely and individualized treatment.
